system_body1: Patient care does not emerge within a single organisation, but through the interaction of multiple system levels. At the macro level, policies, strategies, and funding structures shape the conditions of care. The meso level consists of regional networks and organisations that enable and coordinate collaboration across services. At the micro level, care takes place in practice—where patients, relatives, and healthcare professionals navigate everyday decisions and transitions.
system_body2: The quality of care coordination depends on the interaction between all three levels. Care coordination therefore does not happen in one specific place, but in the handovers between levels—where information, responsibility, and decision-making are passed on.
design_response_lead: Three artefacts that make care coordination visible, structured, and shareable.
artefact1_desc: A shared position paper that makes invisible coordination work visible and creates a common language for socio-medical care coordination.
artefact2_desc: A system-independent handover tool that travels across transitions and helps ensure that what one actor knows, the next one receives.
artefact3_desc: A supporting document that enables information to be shared safely, legally, and ethically across organisations.
hero_sub:
Designing Visible Care Coordination
at Socio-Medical Transitions
hero_desc: A design research project about making invisible care coordination visible at the moments where patients move between healthcare actors, institutions, and responsibilities.
imagine_s1: A doctor follows up on medication.
imagine_s3: A relative clarifies unresolved insurance questions.
imagine_emphasis: But often because people coordinate beyond their formal roles.
imagine_sub: Each contributes to the same care situation, but still works from a different part of the system. This work is called invisible care coordination.
system_lead: Switzerland's healthcare system is highly capable, but fragmented. Care increasingly spans medical treatment, nursing support, social work, relatives, insurers, home care and institutions. When these actors work in parallel without a shared overview, care becomes vulnerable.
sys_macro_tip: Macro — Health policy, financing, cantons, national strategies. The structural framework within which all care is organized.
sys_meso_tip: Meso — Health networks, hospitals, institutions, organisations. Where coordination structures exist — or don't.
sys_micro_tip: Micro — Patients, relatives, GPs, Spitex, coordinators, everyday care practice. Where invisible coordination actually happens.
p1_desc: Mapping the landscape of integrated care and socio-medical coordination in Switzerland. Understanding the system before entering it.
p1_out: → Contextual understanding of fragmented care and the role of coordination as systemic glue.
p2_desc: Going into the field. Listening to healthcare professionals, observing coordination in practice, using COALA as a window into the system.
p2_out: → Rich picture of how coordination actually works — and where it breaks down.
p3_desc: Triangulating findings, mapping the system, reframing the challenge. From structural problem to relational care work.
p3_out: → Area of opportunity: coordination as relational practice, not administrative overhead.
p4_desc: Developing two complementary design interventions through co-design moments, design criteria and iterative prototyping.
p4_out: → Advocacy Paper and Socio-Medical Handover Baton.
p5_desc: Testing interventions with healthcare professionals, gathering feedback. Thinking about implementation, scale, and remaining needs.
p5_out: → Grounded understanding of what the interventions can and cannot do — and what remains to be built.
findings_overall: The problem is not that care coordination is missing; it is that it remains invisible, poorly understood, and therefore difficult to share.
f1: Care coordination is already happening. Not through formal structures, but through invisible work carried by healthcare professionals, relatives, and volunteers.
f2: Coordination already exists, but it cannot be recognised, discussed, shared, or improved because it remains invisible.
f3: Everyone agrees integrated care is important. But when asked what it actually means in everyday practice, people struggle to answer.
f4: Socio-medical transitions are the moments where information, responsibility and care are most likely to fragment.
f5: Coordination depends on relationships before it depends on structures.
f6: The healthcare system optimises individual actors, but rarely the transfer between them.
f7: What becomes visible can become discussable, recognisable and eventually shareable.
f8: The emergence of SOMEKO creates an opportunity to embed care coordination into existing care pathways rather than adding another isolated service.
f9: The Swiss healthcare system works. But often because people coordinate beyond their formal roles. Each contributes to the same care situation, but still works from a different part of the system. This work is called invisible care coordination.
ice_lead: Hover over each item to learn more. The surface shows what is formally recognised. Below the waterline lives the work that keeps care together — invisible, unprotected, and unacknowledged.
ice_a1_tip: Formal meetings between professionals — documented, scheduled, and recognized as part of the care process.
ice_a2_tip: Written referrals between GPs, specialists, and institutions that follow official pathways.
ice_a3_tip: Transfer letters, discharge reports, and formal transition documents.
ice_a4_tip: Officially designated coordinators with a formal mandate — rare, but when present, highly effective.
ice_b1_tip: Personal networks of trust that move information between systems — fast, but fragile and undocumented.
ice_b2_tip: Knowing the right person to call, bending processes to make things work — invisible labour that fills structural gaps.
ice_b3_tip: Patients and families who tell the same story again and again to each new professional — a sign that information does not travel.
ice_b4_tip: Professionals who coordinate more than their job description requires — motivated by care, not mandate.
ice_b5_tip: The cognitive and emotional weight of holding together a care situation no system formally owns.
ice_b6: Knowledge held in one person's head
ice_b6_tip: Critical information about a patient that exists only in the memory of one professional — and disappears when they are absent.
ice_b7_tip: The moment between discharge and intake where no one formally holds the picture — where the baton drops.
rf_before: Coordination as an administrative or structural problem.
rf_pre: Coordination is not an extra service added onto care.
int1_desc: The advocacy paper creates shared language for care coordination. It helps healthcare professionals, organisations and network partners understand why coordination work matters, where it currently remains invisible, and what conditions are needed to recognise it as part of care itself.
int1_p1: Creates a shared vocabulary around coordination
int1_p2: Makes invisible coordination work visible and discussable
int1_p3: Addresses healthcare professionals, networks and institutions
int1_p4: Supports SOMEKO's recognition and embedding in care pathways
int2_desc: The baton is a structured, system-independent coordination tool that travels with the patient across transitions. It supports handovers between actors by making key socio-medical information visible, transferable and shared. It does not replace professional judgement — it creates a shared handover structure so that what one actor knows, the next one receives.
bf1_d: Current health status, primary diagnosis, and immediate needs.
bf2_d: Living situation, family support, social isolation, language barriers.
bf3_d: Who is already involved — formally and informally.
bf4_d: What still needs to happen — and who is responsible.
bf5_d: All professionals and institutions currently contributing to this care situation.
bf6_d: Critical flags that the next actor must know immediately.
bf7_d: Planned actions, upcoming appointments, pending decisions.
bf8_d: Who holds the baton now — and who to reach for questions.
exp_lead: Each marble is one invisible coordination task. Move the patient through the journey and see what happens to the information along the way.
impact_lead: The project does not claim to solve fragmentation alone. Instead, it creates conditions for care coordination to become more visible, shareable and discussable — working at the level of interactions, communication and handovers.
imp7: Bridge between everyday practice and system-level transformation
about_desc: This project was developed as a Bachelor graduation project in Design Management at Lucerne University of Applied Sciences and Arts, in collaboration with the Swiss Center for Design and Health.
about_context: The research was conducted in the Emmental and Oberaargau region of Switzerland, in partnership with SOMEKO, GNE, GNO, COALA, and SCDH.
about_role: Bachelor of Arts · Product and Industrial Design · Specialization in Design Management
about_school: Lucerne University of Applied Sciences and Arts, 2026
contact_desc: Do you work in healthcare, coordination, or system design? Have thoughts on the interventions, the reframing, or the approach? I would love to hear from you.
footer_sub: Designing Visible Care Coordination at Socio-Medical Transitions
